ALL THE THINGS I CAN’T SAY
“I’m a music fan before I’m an artist.” - Cody Frost
Music as a journal, a place to pour it out, to feel, to scream, to rest, and to come back — the only way to make sense of everything that can’t be said aloud. Our digital cover star — Burnley-born musician, illustrator, and tattoo artist Cody Frost — released a new EP, ‘MECHAEVAL’, and also dropped the music video for the track ‘CHRONIC’.

posts.caption:
@gucci has unveiled the new campaign, “Generation Gucci,” shot by @demna himself, presenting the house’s new vision for house. With 84 images, the campaign feels like the opening of a new chapter, introducing new faces and reinterpreting #gucci’s heritage and archival pieces through a modern sensibility.
This is reflected in minimal yet bold silhouettes, alongside Jackie 1961’s new proportions and Dionysus reworked with defined edges. It almost reads as #demna’s statement of respect for the house’s legacy, while simultaneously signalling his vision for a modern Gucci. Swipe right for more campaign images.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @A2Z_FashionMag Now.
📷 @gucci

Two iconic style houses, @dolcegabbana and @rayban, come together to share a unified creative vision, reinterpreting the heritage of both fashion and eyewear.This collaboration reimagines Ray-Ban’s classic Aviator through Dolce&Gabbana’s distinctive lens, resulting in new interpretations of the Shooter and Outdoorsman II, each style available in six colours. The collection features the iconic pilot shape with lightweight metal construction, blending timeless design with a contemporary edge. Captured by Gray Sorrenti, the campaign expresses the vibrant and unapologetic spirit of both brands.
Now available worldwide through both brands’ channels and selected retailers.

posts.caption:
John Waters, the self-styled “Pope of Trash”, turns 80 today. ⁠
⁠
He’s the man who spent his teenage years taking LSD in the suburbs of Baltimore with Divine, who he later directed to eat dog shit in the cult film Pink Flamingoes. He’s also the author of the single best piece of romantic advice ever given: “If you go home with someone and they don’t have any books, don’t fuck them.” So with the latter quip in mind, we looked back on his interview with #CathClarke featured in the S/S14 issue of AnOther Magazine.⁠
⁠
From dealing with bullies to how to stay young, he offers an unconventional guide to life. Read it at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 #JohnWaters. Photography by @mauricehaasphoto
posts.alt_text: Photo by AnOther Magazine on April 22, 2026. May be an image of standing, magazine and text that says 'AnOther John Waters Waters on Staying Young, Regrets and More'.

posts.caption:
Romanticising our past is part of how families tell stories, but photographer and filmmaker @tirtharabinlawati set that aside when he returned to Nepal with stylist @samtthapa, focusing instead on the country’s changing youth culture.⁠
⁠
In 2025, the pair travelled back to the country of their births to create Where We Meet, a project spanning two series shown at Solar Beam Kingdom, curated by Skylar Kang and Jeremy Chen: Girls Skate Nepal, on young girls reshaping skate culture in Kathmandu, and Jamkabhet, tracing underground music scenes and youth communities. ⁠
⁠
“As someone of Nepalese descent but having grown up predominantly in the UK, I’ve always wanted to understand and connect with what the youth were up to over there,” says Thapa. @ohnoitsemilyd speaks with the duo about the project at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Where We Meet. Photography by @tirtharabinlawati. Styling by @samtthapa
posts.alt_text: Photo by AnOther Magazine on April 22, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, magazine, poster and text that says 'AnOther These Photos Photos Capture the Vibrant Spirit of Nepal's Youth Culture'.

posts.caption:
In 1977, in Minneapolis, a 19-year-old musician named Prince Rogers Nelson caught the attention of music agent Owen Husney and his partner Gary Levinson. Keen to have him photographed, they approached @robertwhitmanphoto, playing an early demo of Soft and Wet to draw him in. It worked. Whitman was transfixed, and soon after photographed Nelson, who would become the artist known as Prince.⁠
⁠
Ten years after his passing, we revisit those images collected in Prince Pre Fame, a book that gathers the full series. As Whitman puts it, it’s “a book that every Prince fan should have on their table, telling the story of two guys from Minneapolis following their passions, trying to pursue their dreams.”⁠
⁠
📸 Prince Pre Fame. Photography @robertwhitmanphoto
posts.alt_text: Photo by AnOther Magazine on April 21, 2026. May be a black-and-white image of child, hair, poster, magazine and text that says 'AnOther ther: Candid and Unseen Photos of a Pre- Pre-Fame Fame Prince'.

posts.caption:
The Dutch photographers @inezandvinoodh met in the early 1980s, as students at the Fashion Academy Vogue in Amsterdam. Their first collaboration took place in 1986, when Matadin commissioned Van Lamsweerde to photograph a fashion show for his short-lived clothing line, Lawina. Six years later, the duo fell in love, their romantic relationship taking shape alongside their already-burgeoning artistic partnership. And they haven’t looked back. ⁠
⁠
Four decades on, Inez and Vinoodh are considered two of the most important image-makers of our time. Titled Can Love Be a Photograph?, a new exhibition at @kunstmuseum.nl takes place across 16 rooms and is presented thematically, under titles like ‘(Un)Real’ and ‘The Psychomorphic Phenomenon’, in a way that corresponds to the intimate or expansive nature of each space.⁠
⁠
“We questioned truth and time in photography from day one. We were always excited about being able to disrupt time by going in after the shoot, on the computer, and toying with this idea of reality versus surrealism,” the pair tells @daisyaecw. Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Can Love Be A Photograph? Courtesy of the artists and @kunstmuseum.nl⁠
1. Inez & Vinoodh, Kate, 1999⁠
2. Inez & Vinoodh, Think Love, 2025⁠
4. Inez & Vinoodh, Prince, 2013⁠
5. Inez & Vinoodh, Fashion Plate no. 3, 2020⁠
7. Inez & Vinoodh, A$AP Rocky, RZA & Rihanna – Vogue UK, 2023⁠
⁠
#Inez&Vinoodh

posts.caption:
Between walking runways and fronting campaigns internationally to her silver screen debut in ‘Anyone but You’, @charleefraser is a multi-talented woman. But among Fraser’s manifold skills is one talent that she has, until now, kept on the quiet. “I love roller skating,” she shares. “I’m constantly thinking with the work that I do and [my] schedule. I’m a major organiser – such a Capricorn,” she laughs. “[With] my skates on, the only thing I can think about is what I’m doing. And it just really grounds me, brings me back into that present.”
Through the link in bio, see Charlee Fraser photographed at sunrise on the Northern Beaches of Sydney for BAZAAR.
Charlee Fraser wears @ralphlauren Collection in the April 2026 issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

posts.caption:
“Oh, man, little baby Charlee,” @charleefraser chuckles as she reflects on her career beginnings. “It was really interesting because I wasn’t looking to model at the time – the opportunity kind of presented itself,” she says of her beginning. By 2016, Fraser had been modelling locally for three years, with early gigs including runway appearances for David Jones, Carla Zampatti and Bec + Bridge. “My agency was like, ‘I think it’s time for you to go overseas. You’ve done everything here. Go spread the wings’,” she says. “At the time, I really felt ready for that.”
Continue reading Charlee Fraser’s interview with BAZAAR at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
For #BazaarIndia’s 17th anniversary issue, actor Ananya Panday (@AnanyaPanday) shares that she loves being alone now. “I used to be someone who couldn’t be alone at all. I’d be scared to sit alone for even 10 minutes. I always needed someone around me — my partner, my parents, my friends,” admits Panday. “But when I started living alone, even though I’m in the same building as my parents, that little bit of space changed everything. Building your own environment and doing things your own way has shifted my love for space.”
Much like our cover installation, which conveys the negotiation of the fragile space between two people, the artwork by How Are You Feeling Studio features two film cranes facing each other like figures. Their machine bodies, covered in soft fabric, suggest an unexpected intimacy.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Harper’s Bazaar India’s March cover star Ananya Panday (@AnanyaPanday) is unafraid of being vulnerable. She’s all too aware of the impermanence of fame and of not taking things for granted. “I definitely still have some sense of imposter syndrome. If I hear my name out loud, like someone saying ‘Ananya Panday’ or introducing me somewhere, for a second I kind of disassociate and I can’t fully respond to it,” she admits.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Nita Ambani at the TIME (@time) 100 Summit in New York, in a Swadesh tribal lore saree that reflects the richness of India’s weaving traditions.
The Tribal Lore Jamdani, woven over 24 months by Padma Shri awardee Biren Kumar Basak, brings together meenakari craftsmanship, tribal motifs, and symbolic fish borders—each detail executed with precision.
Its pallu presents a tapestry of ceremonial life, while the body balances colour and composition through finely woven narratives. A piece that moves beyond attire, embodying heritage, artistry, and the enduring language of craft.
#NitaAmbani #RelianceAtTIME100 #TIME100  #TIME100Summit #SwadeshAtTime100

posts.caption:
For most of the social-media era, it’s been an accepted truth that creators with smaller audiences are best at driving sales. But finding those mystical moneymakers was tricky, with an endless number of micro-influencers to choose from and limited data around who actually converts.
That‘s changing fast.
BoF’s Diana Pearl (@dianaspearl) explores how new measurement tools are redefining influencer value — and changing who brands choose to work with. Read more #linkinbio
📷 Unsplash

posts.caption:
Luxury entered 2026 with hopes that new creative directors and signs of stabilisation would finally help the sector turn a corner. Instead, the latest round of earnings has raised bigger questions about what growth now looks like for the industry. While brands including Dior, Gucci and Chanel are generating renewed interest, that excitement has not yet translated into a meaningful sales rebound.
From the slowing Chinese market to geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, luxury conglomerates are facing a complex web of challenges that creative hype alone cannot solve.
On the episode, BoF luxury editors Mimosa Spencer (@mimosaspencer) and Robert Williams (@robert.williams.writes) explain why China remains such a critical missing piece, why Louis Vuitton is under closer scrutiny than usual, and why jewellery continues to outperform the rest of luxury.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheDebriefPodcast
📷 Unsplash
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Business of Fashion on April 23, 2026. May be an image of magazine, signboard, skyscraper, buildings and text that says 'BOF V V DIOR AISTUTION 10415 VUTION at IDUSIUTION DOR PE The Debriet Podcast Why Luxury Still Can't Find Its Way Out of the Slump'.

posts.caption:
Salone fever has rarely burned so bright as it has this year.
No longer just a trade fair, Milan’s Design Week consists of hundreds of installations, exhibitions and gatherings offering a compelling city-sized stage for everyone from Italian design stars to major fashion groups aiming to engage VICs, as well as one of the most effective networking platforms in the creative industries today. It’s no secret that meaningful collaborations (and even major business deals) are born out of conversations that happen here.
Martina Mondadori (@martinamondadori) explores the defining highlights of Milan Design Week. Read more #linkinbio
📷 Tonya Matyu, Andrea Ferrari, Osanna Visconti, Luci Rosse, Loro Piana, Maxime Verret, Frank Frances Studio

posts.caption:
From Brooklyn to Berlin, a growing number of independent store owners are saying no to e-commerce, betting a strong in-store experience can draw customers from their neighbourhood — and beyond.
It’s a direct reaction to the dominant mindset of the 2010s, when online-first retailers like Ssense, Farfetch and Net-a-Porter were booming, and the vast majority of brands at every price point invested heavily in developing their own e-commerce storefronts.
Austin Kim (@austintaekim) explores why some retailers are winning by going offline. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @ven.space, @outlinebrooklyn
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Business of Fashion on April 22, 2026. May be an image of magazine, rack and text that says 'BOF 印館 Ced Meet the Retailers Succeeding by Ignoring the Internet'.

The tie-up between the Rosenfield family, which owns Urban Jürgensen, and Chalamet comes as the traditional watchmaking industry pursues new avenues to widen the appeal of timepieces, including attracting younger audiences.

posts.caption:
Nowadays, it’s not just the party invitation that matters, but the photograph you get when you’re there. Not only are brands investing in more stylistic content, but celebrities, influencers and wealthy shoppers are hiring their own photographers to ensure a steady stream of perfectly staged pictures.
In a fast changing media landscape, where AI is starting to encroach on brand work, working directly with party and event goers is a growing revenue stream for photographers.
BoF’s Joan Kennedy (@joooankennedyyy) explores how event photographers are evolving into on-demand image makers — and why it’s becoming a lucrative new lane. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @germanlarkin, @ryancsides, @byholliem

posts.caption:
Ten years ago today, @beyonce released her pioneering visual album "Lemonade," which solidified the singer and songwriter as one of the most creative and audacious pop stars of her generation. The project also inspired countless trends, including the "Lemonade braids" she wore on the album's cover, her first experimentation with country music in "Daddy Lessons" and a banner season for Red Lobster after she name dropped the chain restaurant in "Formation."
One particular look may define the album: the mustard @roberto_cavalli dress Beyoncé wears to take a retaliatory baseball bat to parked cars, a fire hydrant, a piñata, and shop windows in her fevered, plucky ode to her lover's betrayal, "Hold Up." The slightly sheer cascade of ruffles, with a thin lace of straps at the neck, hardly grabbed attention when it debuted in the Fall 2016 Roberto Cavalli show, which was inspired by 1970s rock 'n roll glamour.
Stylist @bcompleted plucked it from countless yellow dresses however, and on a grinning, retaliatory Beyoncé, its louche frills transformed into a statement of feminine power, freedom and vengeance.
📸: @beyonce; @parkwood
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by CNN Style on April 23, 2026 tagging @bcompleted, @roberto_cavalli, and @beyonce. May be an image of one or more people, makeup and text that says 'CNN'.

posts.caption:
"I was there 19 months," @sabato.desarno said of @gucci, "but I've worked in fashion for 23 years."
After a short but widely scrutinized tenure as creative director at the fashion house — where his restrained aesthetic struggled to gain commercial traction during a broader downturn in the luxury sector — he has been moving across the worlds of design and art with new projects at a more deliberate pace.
Now, during @milan.design.week, he is curating "INSIEME," an exhibition bringing together twelve Italian artisanal companies.
As De Sarno puts it: "I kept thinking about how, when you go to an exhibition or a fair, what you usually see are finished objects. What interested me with 'INSIEME' was turning the attention on everything that comes before that, everything that leads up to the final piece: the mistakes, the decisions, the timing, and those moments when you have to choose a direction and can't go back."
Asked what comes next, De Sarno said: "I see myself simply as Sabato. Someone with different interests, creating conversations with people who are experts in their fields. I'm not an expert in art or design. What I've enjoyed is entering into those conversations and letting projects emerge from them. I don't really feel the need to define it with a title."
📸 : Agnese Bedini/DSL Studio
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by CNN Style on April 22, 2026 tagging @sabato.desarno, and @milan.design.week. May be an image of one or more people, magazine, poster and text that says 'WHAT GUCCI'S FORMER CREATIVE DIRECTOR DID NEXT CNN'.

posts.caption:
Worlds continued to collide at the “Devil Wears Prada 2” premiere in New York, as Vogue’s Anna Wintour and @chloemalle shared a red carpet with Meryl Streep and @annehathaway.
The star-studded event was attended by castmates Emily Blunt and @stanleytucci, as well as @ladygaga, @lucyliu, @cocorocha, @winnieharlow, @simoneashley and @ashleygraham.
Hathaway and Streep set the tone with a devilish color palette that spanned scarlet red, coral and black. Hathaway’s silk bustier gown was a custom @louisvuitton creation, while Streep’s cape coat was @givenchy. Streep, who returns to the screen to reprise her role as the inimitable Runway editor Miranda Priestly, made the look all the more commanding with a pair of black leather opera gloves and blacked-out shades.
📸: Jeenah Moon/Reuters

posts.caption:
For teens during the decade, soundtrack albums were must-have merchandise, PARENTAL ADVISORY labels be damned.
The same year as @bazluhrmann's "Romeo + Juliet," "Trainspotting" came out and @iggypopofficial thumping "Lust for Life" set the pace for another CD that was both achingly cool and a massive commercial success. This was the era of "Dangerous Minds" (1995), "Good Will Hunting" (1997), "Cruel Intentions" (1999), and many more great albums that those of a certain age remember as clearly as the movies themselves.
The "Romeo + Juliet" album itself peaked at No.2 on the Billboard 200 chart and went multi-platinum in several countries. Meanwhile, as Marius de Vries, co-music producer on the film points out, films in the '90s were "regularly spawning enormous hit singles." Think @whitneyhouston's "I Will Always Love You" ("The Bodyguard," 1992) or @celinedion's "My Heart Will Go On" (@titanicmovie, 1997).
In the next decade, the rise of online streaming and death of CDs marked the end of the film soundtrack as we knew it. And while the current wave of '90s nostalgia is partly cyclical, it also taps into a longing for a "much more unified mass culture," said @robharvilla, host of the podcast, "60 Songs That Explain the '90s." In the fragmented internet age, "you don't have that same feeling of unity, where everybody is listening to 'Smells Like Teen Spirit' or 'You Outta Know' at the exact same time."
Without a doubt, the '90s were "a bunch of very golden years," de Vries said. "At the time, we were just having a lot of fun, and I think possibly that was reflected in the music we were making."
📸 : Merrick Morton/20th Century Fox/Shutterstock/Moviestore Collection Ltd/Alamy Stock Photo/Ronald Grant/Everett Collection
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by CNN Style on April 20, 2026 tagging @bazluhrmann, and @clairedanes. May be an image of poster, portable cassette player and text that says 'THE '90S MOVIE SOUNDTRACK THA STILL MAKES MILLENNIAL HEARTS FLUTTER CNN'.

posts.caption:
Citlali Fabián has been named Photographer of the Year at the Sony World Photography Awards 2026, with the judging panel hailing her photo series "Bilha, Stories of my Sisters," which focuses on Indigenous activists and artists in southern Mexico.
Fabián, who is from the Yalalteca Indigenous community, "uses photography to explore ways of addressing identity and its connections with territory, migration, and community bonds," said the organizers in a statement.
Alongside Fabián, winners and finalists were chosen across various categories in the professional competition. They include Joy Saha in the architecture and design category; Santiago Mesa in the documentary projects category; and Isadora Romero in the environment category.
The competition, which is now in its 19th year, received more than 430,000 images from more than 200 countries and territories, according to the organizers.
A selection of images from the awards will be displayed at Somerset House in London from April 17 to May 4.
See more images at the link in our bio.
📸: Sony World Photography Awards/ Citlali Fabián; Seungho Kim; Gargi Sharma; Will Burrard-Lucas; Jean-Marc Caimi & Valentina Piccinni; Pablo Ramos; Dafna Talmor; Isadora Romero

posts.caption:
There are influencers, and then there’s @madelineargy. Since 2021, the “Pretty Lonesome” host has made eight million strangers feel like her closest friends without even trying. Whether she’s giving advice on toxic friendships or crashing out over Jane Goodall, it’s clear that Argy’s got range—and so does her closet.
Coveteur went inside the closet of her LA home and found rows of candy-colored @chanelofficial bags on one wall and thrifted tanks from @thredup and @depop on the other.

posts.caption:
Madonna’s style has always leaned toward the bold, but it’s rarely careless. Her looks are usually clearly thought through, sometimes provocative, sometimes more restraine.
Over time, they’ve become less about fashion alone and more about how she presents herself in a frame.
One of the more notable examples is the cemetery shoot. The contrast is immediate: lingerie layered under a trench coat, heels, sheer tights, and flowers in hand, all set against a graveyard. It’s an unusual combination, and it creates a strong visual clash between setting and styling. Still, it doesn’t come across as random. The whole scene feels arranged, like she’s fully aware of the tension it creates and is working with it rather than against it.
Then there’s the red background image with the black sequined dress. It’s much cleaner in comparison. The silhouette is simple, the styling is minimal, with short blonde hair and a steady, direct pose. There’s no real attempt at excess here. Instead, it feels precise and structured, the kind of image that stands out because of its clarity.
The black leather outfit takes an even more stripped-back approach. Gloves, heels, fishnets, sunglasses, all fairly straightforward pieces, worn with a relaxed confidence. She’s seated, composed, and the attitude does most of the work. Nothing feels added for effect.
Across these different looks, what remains consistent is the way she presents them. The clothes change, but the sense of control doesn’t.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on April 23, 2026. May be an image of costume, dress, poster and text that says 'Madonna' AH Time, Most Iconic Outfits. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
In Tim Walker’s “Check-Mate” editorial for Vogue Italia (December 2015), everything feels a bit unreal, like stepping into a strange version of a fairytale.
Edie Campbell moves through different sets that look like they’re taken from a dream. There are chessboards, oversized props, bold colors, and details that feel slightly off, but in a way that still looks visually right. It’s playful, but not in a childish way, more like a mix between fantasy and high fashion.
The clothes shift between structured couture and more exaggerated, almost costume-like pieces. Some looks are very clean and tailored, others are more dramatic, but they all fit into the same world. Nothing feels random, even when it looks unusual.
Hair and makeup stand out in a subtle but important way. Light brows, strong eye looks, and unexpected color choices make everything feel a bit more surreal without taking attention away from the clothes.
Overall, it’s the kind of editorial that doesn’t try too hard to explain itself. It’s more about the mood, the visuals, and how everything comes together naturally.
Credits:
Photography: Tim Walker
Styling: Jacob Kjeldgaard
Model: Edie Campbell
Makeup: Miranda Joyce
Hair: Shon
Editorial: “Check-Mate”, Vogue Italia, December 2015
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on April 22, 2026. May be an image of chess and text that says '13 This Editorial feels like falling down the rabbit hole. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
At Alessandro Michele’s Gucci FW2018 Cyborg show, during Milan Fashion Week, the runway was presented inside a „highly controlled“, clinical environment designed to resemble an operating theater. The set was defined by sterile green-turquoise walls, harsh overhead lighting, stainless surfaces, and hospital-style beds, creating a setting that felt detached from traditional fashion spectacle and closer to a staged experiment.
Within this environment, models moved at a measured pace through the space, each look constructed with extreme density and precision. The collection itself merged multiple visual languages at once: historical tailoring, ecclesiastical references, sportswear influences, and highly decorative elements were layered together in a way that resisted any single interpretation. Accessories and props extended the garments into sculptural forms, including hyper-realistic replicas of human heads and hybrid, creature-like objects that disrupted the boundary between body and object.
Beauty direction reinforced this constructed logic. Hair was sculptural and intentionally artificial in finish, while makeup emphasized transformation rather than enhancement, pushing the idea that identity here is something assembled rather than inherent.
Seen in hindsight, the collection aligns closely with contemporary conversations around identity as a layered, mediated construct, anticipating the visual and conceptual codes that now dominate digital fashion culture.
Credits :
Creative Director: Alessandro Michele
Casting Director: Maida Gregori Boina
Set Design: Gideon Ponte
Production Design: Gucci ArtLab / Gucci Creative Team
Makeup: Pat McGrath
Hair: Guido Palau
Special Effects / Props: Makinarium
Music: Steve Mackey
Runway Production: Art + Commerce / Gucci Team
Inspiration: Donna Haraway – A Cyborg Manifesto
Runway Photography: Sonny Vandevelde / Indigital.tv
Press: Gucci Press Office

posts.caption:
Nina Hagen is a German singer, songwriter, and actress who first appeared in East Berlin before rising to fame in the late 1970s punk scene. She became known for her extreme voice, theatrical performances, and completely unpredictable public image. Early on she worked as an actress and later moved into music, where she mixed punk, opera, and performance art in a way nobody else was really doing at the time. She quickly became one of the most recognizable and unconventional figures in European punk culture and is still often called the “Godmother of Punk.” ￼
What makes Nina Hagen stand out is not just her music, but her personality. She has always been loud, expressive, and completely unfiltered, often mixing political, spiritual, and social ideas into her public presence. She never really fit into one category, which is exactly what made her interesting in the first place. ￼
In terms of feminism and fashion, Nina Hagen became an early symbol of anti-conformity. In a scene that was already breaking rules, she pushed it even further by using her body, voice, and style as a form of resistance. Punk fashion in general was about rejecting mainstream beauty standards, and she embodied that fully with exaggerated looks, bold makeup, and constantly changing identities. ￼
Her impact on feminism isn’t about slogans, but about presence. She showed that a woman in music didn’t have to be polished, quiet, or “acceptable” to be powerful. Her entire aesthetic and attitude became part of a wider movement where women in punk were not just performers, but central forces shaping the culture itself.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on April 21, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'The Godmother of ofPunk, Nina Hagen. CULT'.

posts.caption:
In Tim Walker’s “boy/girl/boy” editorial for Vogue Italia (July 2016), fashion stops behaving like fashion in the traditional sense and becomes something more fluid, almost cinematic.
Stella Tennant and James Crewe are placed in this soft, slightly surreal world where gender doesn’t feel defined in a strict way. Instead, it’s all about mood, attitude, and the way clothes sit on the body. Tailoring shifts between sharp and relaxed, masculine and feminine blends without ever feeling forced or explained.
What stands out is how calm it all is. Nothing feels like a statement piece or a big idea being pushed. It’s more subtle than that. You just get the sense that the usual rules around dressing aren’t really important here.
Tim Walker builds a visual story that feels almost like a dream. It doesn’t try to be current or trend-driven, which is probably why it still feels so relevant today. It just exists in its own space and lets the clothes and people speak for themselves.
Credits:
Photography: Tim Walker
Styling: Jacob K
Models: Stella Tennant, James Crewe
Makeup: Miranda Joyce
Hair: Shon
Editorial: “boy/girl/boy”, Vogue Italia, July 2016

An internet girl to her core, @sofiabadi first found her way into fashion during her early 20s, when she created a Twitter account to participate in the burgeoning hftwt (high fashion Twitter) community, and quickly became one of its central figures. ⁠
⁠
“I’ve always been an opinionated person, and that’s something that I think drew people into my fashion commentary,” she says“But they ruined my favourite place on Earth. It’s heartbreaking because I don’t want to make Reels and TikToks. There was something so inherently erotic about Twitter.”⁠
⁠
The 28-year-old Argentinian designer has built a cult following online through her dreamy pieces that evoke the charm of endless sleepovers, hanging out with your best friends, and playing dress-up in your older sister’s closet. ⁠
⁠
Se’s also caught the eye of celebrities including Rosalía, Grimes, and Twice member Chaeyoung.⁠
⁠
Tap the link in bio to read her interview with Dazed on the Buenos Aires creative scene, her upcoming plans for a couture line, and her secret styling hacks.⁠

posts.caption:
The natural diamond industry isn’t declining. It’s being redefined. 💎
At a powerful roundtable during IIJS Bharat Signature 2026, industry leaders unpacked a hard truth:
This is no longer a cyclical slowdown. It’s a structural reset.
From shrinking market share and shifting consumer priorities to the rise of lab-grown diamonds and digital-first retail, the rules have changed.
But here’s where it gets interesting:
✨ Supply is tightening
✨ High-end demand is growing
✨ Consumers are still willing to spend just differently
The real challenge?
Rebuilding aspiration, relevance and emotional connection.
Because at its core, this industry has never been about product.
It’s always been about the dream.
If you’re in the diamond business, 2026 isn’t about waiting for recovery.
It’s about reinvention.
What do you think the industry needs most right now better branding or better pricing?
#DiamondWorld #NaturalDiamonds #DiamondIndustry #DWMagazine #RetailStrategy

posts.caption:
Predator turned born-again British MAGA stan Russell Brand is under fire (again) after casually admitting on Megyn Kelly’s podcast that he slept with a 16-year-old.
“The plain fact of it is that in Europe and in the United Kingdom, where I’m from, the age of consent is 16, and I did sleep with a 16-year-old when I was 30,” he said. “But when I was 30, I was a very different person. I was a lot younger, and I was an immature 30-year-old.” 🤮
Kelly also raised new sexual assault allegations involving his ex-wife Katy Perry by Ruby Rose, which the singer has denied. “This is probably the old school man in me... I don’t even hear the crime there.”
Brand, 50, is currently facing seven rape and sexual assault charges involving seven women tied to alleged incidents between 1999 and 2009. He has denied all charges.
Meanwhile, he’s spent recent years seemingly aiming for a redemption arc by speaking at multiple Turning Point USA events, cozying up to MAGAts like Dr. Oz and Worm Brain Jr., even receiving a “Global Defender of Freedom” award at Mar-a-Lago last year.
Nothing says moral rebirth quite like rebranding before trial, which begins in October.
#news #trial #threads #reels #fyp
posts.alt_text:
“Who is the Bleu de Chanel man?” Chanel asked yesterday on IG, as a silhouetted figure of their new fragrance ambassador appeared on their feed. 

Well, it definitely wasn’t Timothée Chalamet, who took on the Bleu de Chanel ambassador role in 2023 after the fragrance’s first ever ambassador, Gaspard Ulliel. Ulliel had held the role for twelve years from Bleu de Chanel’s release in 2010 to his tragic death in 2022. 

This morning, it was confirmed that Jacob Elordi would replace Chalamet. The announcement features various shots of the actor in profile, highlighting his famously sharp jawline and shaggy mullet. The clip ends on a shot of him pointing straight into the camera with two fingers. In other words, it’s highly masculine, authoritative, and a bit playful in contrast to the minimalistic and soft announcement for Chalamet just a few years ago. 

It makes sense: Elordi has built an illustrious reputation that transcends his breakout TV role in Euphoria. He has an Oscar nom under his belt, has starred alongside Margot Robbie, who is also a Chanel ambassador and was featured with Elordi in an ad for Chanel No. 5 last year. Chanel describes him as “a deeply determined man, driven by his instinct.” 

For reference, Chalamet’s announcement in 2023 was much less specific, stating only that Bleu was “a fragrance for the man who is deeply himself.” Perhaps that was Chalamet’s selling point at the time. Then again, perhaps that was Bleu de Chanel’s selling point at the time. What changed? 

Since Chalamet’s appointment at Chanel, he graduated from the boyish, French-ish heartthrob with the pretty loose curls and the gender-bending outfits, becoming instead a devil-may-care troublemaker with a taste for hype styling and awkward facial hair. And, the charm that made him beloved in his early career all but wore off completely when he made incendiary comments about opera and ballet last February. Chanel, one Threads user notes, is a “major patron” of the Paris Opera Ballet. Could these be related?

Further, will two or three stints be the norm for Chanel’s fragrance ambassadorships? Or can Ulliel’s longstanding role be repeated with Elordi?

In any case, Congrats Jacob!

posts.caption:
“Who is the Bleu de Chanel man?” Chanel asked yesterday on IG, as a silhouetted figure of their new fragrance ambassador appeared on their feed.
Well, it definitely wasn’t Timothée Chalamet, who took on the Bleu de Chanel ambassador role in 2023 after the fragrance’s first ever ambassador, Gaspard Ulliel. Ulliel had held the role for twelve years from Bleu de Chanel’s release in 2010 to his tragic death in 2022.
This morning, it was confirmed that Jacob Elordi would replace Chalamet. The announcement features various shots of the actor in profile, highlighting his famously sharp jawline and shaggy mullet. The clip ends on a shot of him pointing straight into the camera with two fingers. In other words, it’s highly masculine, authoritative, and a bit playful in contrast to the minimalistic and soft announcement for Chalamet just a few years ago.
It makes sense: Elordi has built an illustrious reputation that transcends his breakout TV role in Euphoria. He has an Oscar nom under his belt, has starred alongside Margot Robbie, who is also a Chanel ambassador and was featured with Elordi in an ad for Chanel No. 5 last year. Chanel describes him as “a deeply determined man, driven by his instinct.”
For reference, Chalamet’s announcement in 2023 was much less specific, stating only that Bleu was “a fragrance for the man who is deeply himself.” Perhaps that was Chalamet’s selling point at the time. Then again, perhaps that was Bleu de Chanel’s selling point at the time. What changed?
Since Chalamet’s appointment at Chanel, he graduated from the boyish, French-ish heartthrob with the pretty loose curls and the gender-bending outfits, becoming instead a devil-may-care troublemaker with a taste for hype styling and awkward facial hair. And, the charm that made him beloved in his early career all but wore off completely when he made incendiary comments about opera and ballet last February. Chanel, one Threads user notes, is a “major patron” of the Paris Opera Ballet. Could these be related?
Further, will two or three stints be the norm for Chanel’s fragrance ambassadorships? Or can Ulliel’s longstanding role be repeated with Elordi?
In any case, Congrats Jacob!

posts.caption:
The Devil Wears Prada 2 press tour was packed with some of Anne Hathaway and Meryl Streep’s most fabulous fashion moments to date, culminating in an ultra chic NYC premier earlier this week. But the big DWP news now is that Sydney Sweeney was axed from the film.
In the long awaited sequel, Runway magazine is in trouble. So, Miranda Preistley must bring together her trusty team to get things back in order. That team includes her right-hand man Nigel Kipling, her former assistant Andy Sachs, and, lastly, Emily Charlton, who is now the head of Dior US. In the cut scene, Nigel, Andy, and Miranda would have descended upon the Dior offices to enlist Emily’s help, only to find her knee-deep in a fitting with a celebrity. That celebrity would have been Sydney Sweeney as herself.
Entertainment Weekly reports that the scene was edited out because it “did not work structurally.” But, the decision to nix Sweeney comes at a pretty coincidental moment. She is still in hot water after her eugenics-ridden American Eagle ad last year, and has only leaned into the controversy in subsequent interviews. Since then, she has courted more controversy gleefully, staging a faux stunt at the Hollywood sign to promote her lingerie line and playing a provocative role in Euphoria season 3. Her actions have been lauded by Trump and she appears to have become an unofficial symbol of ideal femininity for conservative white-supremacist men.
Even before the news of her cut DWP2 scene, audiences have been speculating on the toxicity of Sweeney’s brand. The actor often wears vintage pieces on red carpets, causing fashion insiders to believe that brands refuse to dress her. Others called for the cancellation of Euphoria after Sweeney appeared in a pornographic baby outfit.
It’s a little hard to believe that Sweeney was cut from DWP2 because of structural issues. It’s not like casting Sweeney, who reportedly makes millions for on-screen appearances these days, is a throwaway decision. Then again, we are talking about Runway mag, where Miranda Preistley once canceled the autumn jacket shoot to pull up the Sedona shoot. And we all know how much THAT cost.

Trump's favorite leading lady will not appear in the sequel amid a rotating door of scandals. @brinzaraa THE EVIL HAS BEEN DEFEATED Diet PRADA'.

posts.caption:
The fashions seen over the last two Coachella weekends were far from the eccentric, highly curated, culturally-appropriative bohemian glam looks we expected to see. In fact, they were the total opposite.
Stars like Kendall Jenner, Hailey Baldwin, Amber Rose, and Vanessa Hudgens wrote the playbook for how to dress at Coachella: Feathers in your hair, tribal headdresses and flower crowns, macrame crop-tops, and Navajo prints. Coachella style was deeply problematic, it was plastered all over Tumblr, and it was so codified that it became its own retail category: the annual “festival collection.”
But now, the same celebs are turning against it. Kendall Jenner was seen this year in basic tank tops and baggy jorts. Katy Perry and Justin Trudeau’s white t-shirt stunned the internet. And Hailey Beiber, the first lady of Beiberchella, wore only a vintage windbreaker one weekend, and a hoodie with running shorts the next weekend.
There is a lot influencing this: We spotted indie sleaze style reflected in Gabbriette’s Chrome Hearts looks. Amber Rose’s shorts, Hailey Bieber’s and Emma Chamberlain’s leather boots, and Amelia Gray’s stiff straight-cut denim all echoed fashion’s growing interest in workwear. And, the quiet luxury movement informed almost all these outfits being that each came with surreptitious designer tags.
But also, this movement toward underdressing took place under the giant YouTube screen of Beiberchella, which was, by the way, similarly minimal and ad hoc. Erego, this isn’t just a fashion issue (it never is!) but is indicative of a larger trend that is skeptical of overproduction and is nostalgic for authenticity. Perhaps it’s a foil to the onslaught of product placements, highly styled appearances, and manufactured moments that have overtaken Coachella.
Celebrity underdressers appear to be offering counterpoint, a way to distance themselves from the masses who are clawing for visibility at music fests. Or perhaps they’re reaching for the styles that made music festivals so iconic in the first place, back when it was common to be a little messy. Dirty, even. A la Kate Moss at Glastonbury. Is Coachella underdressing actually a breath of fresh air?
posts.alt_text: Photo by Diet Prada ™ on April 20, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'RIP Overdressing at Coachella Celebrities and seasoned Coachella-goers embraced underdressing in contrast to their styling in former years. Diet PRADA'.

posts.caption:
Hallmark Channel dropped the promo poster for its new movie ‘All’s Fair in Love and Mahjong’ on Friday and the internet wasted no time dragging it.
For a movie centered around mahjong, people are side-eyeing the casting choices. The poster shows a mostly white cast, with just one Asian actor, Yan-Kay Crystal Lowe, who is of Chinese-Scottish descent.
The rom-com synopsis reads: “A school nurse turns her love of the game Mahjong into a new path, inspired by a jovial contractor fixing up her home, who encourages her to take a chance on the future.”
Meanwhile, the mahjong tiles featured on the poster are from the film’s branded set, which Hallmark plans to sell on its website through a collab with Miss Mahjong. The Kansas-based game company says it was founded by “a trio of Mahjong-obsessed friends and neighbors” named Anne Marie, Morgan, and Betsy. Although the founders don’t share additional details (🧐) the optics are giving the same energy as a the whitewashed mahjong brands we previously called out: Oh My Mahjong! (just three weeks ago) and The Mahjong Line back in 2021.
The film’s title, a spin on the phrase, “all’s fair in love and war,” was probably meant to be cheeky, but instead the comments section seems ready for battle with the network.
“Just do monopoly next time or something,” wrote one person. “Collective ancestral sigh,” wrote another.
To top it off, the movie is set to premiere May 9… during AAPI Heritage Month. @hallmarkchannel it’s not too late to scrap this mess.
#mahjong #movie #film #aapi #viral

posts.caption:
California’s race for governor is undergoing a major political shakeup after Eric Swalwell’s campaign imploded almost overnight, going from leading candidate to disgraced ex-congressman. Multiple women have accused him of sexual assault and harassment, which he denies. With a criminal investigation now hanging over him, Swalwell dropped out Monday and resigned from Congress, leaving the top spot suddenly wide open.
There are still seven Dems in the running, including Xavier Becerra, Tony Thurmond, Betty Yee, Matt Mahan, and Antonio Villaraigosa. But right now, the frontrunner is looking like climate activist Tom Steyer who is leading with 21% in a SurveyUSA poll conducted last week as Swalwell’s scandal broke, followed by former Rep. Katie Porter at 8%.
On the campaign trail last week, Steyer pointed out gas in downtown LA reached $8.29/gal, adding, “The working people of California are paying for Donald Trump’s war in Iran.”
Apparently that hit a nerve. By Monday, Trump was throwing (another) tantrum on Truth Social. “Why is FoxNews showing SLEAZEBAG Tom Steyer, doing a big piece on him right now, and probably putting him “into play,” instead of talking about Republican Candidates, and really good ones like Steve Hilton, and Republicans?,” he whined.
“Nobody cares about Tom Steyer, he is a LOSER, but Fox is making it possible for a guy like this to win. VOTE FOR STEVE HILTON, AND TURN CALIFORNIA AROUND!”
The GOP hasn’t had a CA governor since Schwarzenegger left in 2011, but they see a potential opening as Steve Hilton stands at 18%, according to the same poll.
Under the state’s top-two primary system, the two candidates with the most votes will run in November, regardless of party. So yes, it’s still anyone’s race and the June 2 primary is around the corner. Mark your calendars!
#california #news

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: There are artists who arrive with noise, and then there are artists like Zayn Malik (@zayn), the kind who never really needed to raise his voice to command attention. Some people have presence; Malik has always had pull. Fifteen years after his debut, and nearly a decade into his solo career, Malik returns to ELLE India, where he first made history in 2018 with his first-ever Indian magazine cover. There’s no forced “full circle” narrative here, no overworked nostalgia. If anything, this moment feels less like a return and more like a continuation — the same artist, only more settled into himself. “I don’t feel like I’m at such a different place in my career,” he says. “I feel like I’m at a different place and point as a human; I’m developing, growing, and learning from life experiences.” That distinction feels important. The artistry was never in question. If anything, it is the life around it that now feels more fully aligned. Head to the 🔗in bio to read the full conversation with our cover star.

posts.caption:
#ELLEExclusive: Judith Leiber Couture has officially arrived in India, marking a significant moment in the country’s growing luxury landscape. With its first store at The Chanakya Mall in New Delhi, the brand brings over six decades of craftsmanship, precision, and unmistakable glamour to a new audience.
Known for turning evening bags into collectible works of art, Judith Leiber has long existed at the intersection of fashion and art. From its signature crystal-encrusted minaudières to sculptural, statement-making designs, the brand’s universe is defined by whimsy, detail, and bold expression.
For its India debut, the offering blends globally iconic pieces with designs that feel closer to home—playful, intricate creations inspired by motifs like elephants, parrots, and florals.
The move signals more than just a store opening. It reflects India’s rising influence as a key luxury market, driven by a new generation that values individuality, craftsmanship, and standout design.
#JudithLeiber #ELLEFashion

posts.caption:
@oliviadeano is everywhere—and for good reason.
Since the September release of her hit sophomore album, ‘The Art of Loving,’ the British songstress has been riding a serious wave of momentum on both sides of the pond. Add in a Grammy win, standout live performances, a @cartier campaign, and critical raves, and it’s clear: Dean’s starpower is undeniable. As she prepares to kick off the album’s sold-out world tour, Dean is trying her best to soak it all in—and define success on her own terms.
In ELLE’s Women in Music cover story, @sylviaobell chats with the Grammy winner about navigating public opinion in her newfound spotlight, the wisdom the women in her life have imparted on her, and the precedent she hopes to set with her upcoming tour: “I don’t think you need to be someone who’s got loads of money to enjoy your favorite album.” Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
@elizabethbanks isn't slowing down.
After years of delivering memorable performances in films such as ‘Wet Hot American Summer,’ ‘Pitch Perfect,’ and ‘The Hunger Games,’ the actress has woven her way inextricably through the Hollywood system while taking on numerous other roles: producer, director, advocate, and entrepreneur. All the while, she's maintained a steady presence in front of the camera; this month, she's back on our screens alongside Matthew Macfadyen in @peacock's ‘The Miniature Wife.’
“I want to be impactful,” Banks tells ELLE. “And as an artist, I don’t make art in a closet for myself. I am trying to convey something to the audience. I’m trying to speak to the time that I live in. I’m trying to make art that moves people, entertains people, and educates people.”
Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption: Let it be known: @demimoore is no Elisabeth Sparkle. Despite playing a character obsessed with youth, the Oscar-nominated actress is interested in longevity—not turning back the clock. “It’s a cautionary tale about what happens when you try to cheat nature rather than nurture it,” she tells ELLE. As the face of @lancomeofficial and Longevity MD’s Reset line, Moore hopes others forego resisting time, but instead take the time to understand our skin’s biology and give it the advanced tools it needs to thrive. Read more from our interview with Moore at the link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Photo by ELLE Magazine on April 22, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, makeup, magazine and text that says 'ELLE Demi Moore Shares Her Longevity and Wellness andWellnessSecrets Secrets'.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Shakaila Forbes-Bell on October 14, 2025 tagging @secondhand_pigeon, and @fashionispsychology.

posts.caption:
Lululemon named Heidi O’Neill as the athleisure company’s new CEO, effective Sept. 8.
The news comes after the company has seen more than a year of disappointing performance and is embroiled in a dramatic proxy battle, with founder Chip Wilson criticizing the business.
Shares of the company sank more than 5% in extended trading.
O’Neill has held multiple roles at Nike, contributing to the sportswear behemoth’s growth. She also held positions at Levi Strauss, Hyatt Hotels and Spotify.

posts.caption:
California released Amazon’s internal emails on Monday. They are worth reading carefully if you are a fashion brand that sells on the platform.
The short version: Amazon identified products listed at lower prices on Walmart and Target, contacted its vendors, and asked them to get those prices raised. The vendors complied. Levi Strauss confirmed Walmart had raised the price of its khaki trousers to $29.99 the same day Amazon asked. Hanes contacted both Walmart and Target directly after Amazon sent it links to cheaper competitor listings. California Attorney General Rob Bonta is calling this price-fixing. Read more in this column by Editor-in-Chief Anuj Kumar on fashionlawjournal.com

KL Rahul brings his understated approach to style to the forefront with his latest collaboration with Paul & Shark for Spring/Summer 2026. Designed with a focus on ease and refinement, the capsule blends Italian craftsmanship with Rahul’s signature minimal, no-fuss aesthetic.
Rooted in versatility, the collection reimagines classics through a contemporary lens—from Bandhgala-inspired details to breathable knits that subtly nod to his cricketing identity. Think lightweight layers, elevated essentials, and a palette that moves between soft neutrals and muted statement tones.
At its core, the drop reflects a shared philosophy: style that speaks quietly but carries weight - where comfort, craftsmanship, and confidence do all the talking.

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 08, 2026.

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about ten things Good On You has accomplished in the past ten years. Share how you think the sustainable fashion and beauty industry have shifted in the past decade 💚⁠

posts.caption:

posts.caption:
What are your hopes this #EarthDay? ⁠
⁠
We’re thinking about the need to protect, defend, and honor what makes this planet so special: the environments that give back more than we take from it, the creatures big and small who maintain biodiversity, and people like you who are working toward a kinder life for all 💚⁠
⁠
Good On You exists to help keep people like you informed, to empower you in your journey to reduce your impact, and to keep others informed on how we can reduce our impact. Each of us has an obligation to honour and protect the workers, the nature, and the animals impacted by the objects in our lives. How can we be better for them? 🌠⁠
⁠
Share how you plan to continue to make change and why you continue to show up for the planet year after year. Together, we can change an industry like fashion, to get them to understand the need to conserve instead of capitalise: learn more about the planet and fashion’s impact at the link ↑⁠
⁠
📸 @nasa⁠
⁠
#Nature #Sustainability #SustainableFashion #GoodOnYouApp⁠

posts.caption:
Do you get caught in a loop of anxious thoughts? Play mistakes over and over in your mind, or come away from social situations and ruminate over something you said that might have caused offence? Do you worry about all the ways a situation may get worse, or can’t shake the thought you’re not quite measuring up? If so, you’re probably a binge thinker.
Binge thinking - overthinking - has been described by @drjessamy as ’doom scrolling for the brain,’ and, of course, women are more susceptible to it than men. But how do we bust out of the binge thinking trap?
Swipe to read our tips on how to cut down your overthinking, and tap the link in bio to read more.

posts.caption:
@chloessevigny is the real deal. For three decades, Sevigny has cut a singular path, both on screen and in fashion, building a body of work that feels as unexpected as it does authentically her own. In Bazaar’s May 2026 issue, the actor talks about her next move—“The Five-Star Weekend,” a dramatic miniseries adapted from the Elin Hilderbrand novel in which she stars alongside Jennifer Garner. It is not the kind of project that one would immediately associate with Sevigny, but that’s precisely why it also makes sense; she’ll never fit neatly into any box. “My agents sent it to me, and I was like, ‘This is too mainstream. My fans are not going to like it,’” Sevigny says, “but I really feel there’s really something for everybody.”
-

posts.caption:
What @chloessevigny stands for and what she’s stood for since the ’90s is a kind of preternatural authenticity, an unwavering sense of self. She became a natural emblem for the Alt girl. As Grunge took off, Sevigny realized, “Oh, people do think I’m something, and I want to take advantage of it in a way that is beneficial for me too.’” Even then, Sevigny knew that modeling would not give her the creative freedom and control over her career that she wanted for herself. “I remember doing the X-girl fashion show [in 1994] and seeing everybody in the audience [and thinking] I wanted to be one of the creative types that was invited to watch the show and not one of the girls walking it. It was a crystallizing moment for me.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
-

posts.caption:
@chloessevigny's story in the public eye is as tied to personal style as it is to her acting, and she’s managed to sustain it for more than 30 years in fits that have never missed. On the red carpet, Sevigny is known for mixing überluxury labels like @YSL, @Gucci, and @MiuMiu with of-the-moment and under-the-radar brands like @giovannaflor3s or @AugustBarron. “Everyone’s always like, ‘Chloë’s style!’ but it’s like, none of you even know my style because you don’t see me every day! There’s very few paparazzi [shots] of me day in, day out or me going out to Sway once a week. Nobody sees all those outfits, sadly, and they’re really good.” At the link in bio, Sevigny talks fashion and returning to the @miumiu runway after 30 years.

posts.caption:
@chloessevigny is figuring out how to keep up her innate way of being in the world. “I think it’s going to be just experiencing things, you know what I mean?” she says. “It’s not going to happen in the phone.” Living through Covid and becoming a mom complicated things. “I need to find the essence of why I love things again because it’s getting very clouded,” she says. “I was in a cycle of going to the movies, art shows, reading books, listening to new music…and then it was Covid, and then I had a child, and now I feel like I’m just outside of all of that.” Sevigny opens up to Bazaar’s deputy culture director Laia Garcia-Furtado (@geometricsleep) about finding ways to stay curious at the link in bio.
-

posts.caption:
What happens when you use a material famous for being as light as air to recreate a slab of solid rock? Meet “Airstone.”
Now on view and available to shop at Via Santa Marta 10 in Milan’s 5VIE district, the new Airstone tote bag is a design paradox.
Designed by @nichettostudio, the bag’s form was originally hand-carved from a block of rock before being 3D-scanned and injected with @XLEXTRALIGHT technology.
The result? A primitive-futuristic, limited-edition accessory that looks like a heavy, jagged artifact but weighs next to nothing. It’s a sensory mind-game that challenges how we perceive mass and material memory.
Sustainability isn’t an afterthought here, either.
The bag is crafted using XL EXTRALIGHT® Sustainable+, a blend featuring 51% post-industrial waste.
To launch the Airstone, XL EXTRALIGHT® has opened a popup installation which mixes acid-yellow accents, industrial metal cages, and sculptural foam rocks. It’ll have you wondering whether you’re in a high-end boutique or a geology lab.
The Airstone tote is available in three colorways exclusively at the Milan Design Week pop-up at Via Santa Marta 10.

posts.caption:
A Louisiana designer @noahvlaye created set made from “paper textile.” ⁠
⁠
The look mimics notebook paper through clever stitching — reddish-pink vertical lines paired with light blue horizontal ones — brought to life using layered white T-shirt fabrics, some faded or yellowed to evoke aged pages.⁠
⁠
Originally made two years ago, the piece is being revisited to highlight its concept and construction, offering a simple but inventive idea that others might build on or reinterpret.

posts.caption:
@naomigscott has spent years bringing other people’s stories to life. Now, with her debut album ‘F.I.G.’, she’s finally telling her own. From Disney teen to big screen star, she’s moved fluidly between worlds, always compelling but rarely the one in control of the narrative. Now the artist is stepping into her own, stripping everything back to offer an intimate and unfiltered look at who she is beyond the screen.
The album is a genre-fluid odyssey that moves between gospel warmth, ‘80s pop nostalgia, alt-pop edge and soulful ‘90s R&B. Made in a creative bubble that Scott describes as instinct-driven and free, it doesn’t sound like a debut so much as an arrival.
With her first North American tour on the horizon, we sat down with Scott to talk self-expression, musical identity and what it means to finally be seen as herself. Head to the link in bio to read the full interview.
Credits: @eloise_parry, Scott Jeremie Levy

posts.caption:
Would you believe us if we told you the scent of women’s tears makes men less aggressive? After seeing some serious statistics from Scientific American, creatives Carlota Real and Natalia Zapata decided to turn that data into a weapon.
Introducing “Tearqualizer”, the first product of its kind designed to combat everything from mansplaining to fragile egos. “The solution to toxic masculinity is not milking our tears,” the duo explains. “But microaggressions are so out of hand that we created an equally insane solution to ask… is this really what it takes?”
“Tearqualizer” isn’t available for purchase yet, but the need for it is very much real. Would you buy it, or dare we ask, make your own?
Credit: Carlota Real and Natalia Zapata

posts.caption:
It might sound extreme to say that fashion is a reflection of the state of the world, but it’s true, and few brands embody this as powerfully as @litkovska_official. The Ukrainian label staged its first runway show at Paris Fashion Week and delivered a deeply emotional show that drew directly from the realities of war. The collection was an honest spotlight on resilience, hardship and hope. We caught up with founder Lilia Litkovska to talk about heritage, creativity and designing through conflict. Head to the link in bio for the full interview.
Images: Courtesy of Brand

posts.caption:
Beach bound? 🏝️The Summer Series from @jw_anderson delivers the designer’s craft-centered ethos to a beach-ready collection all about open toes and tactile totes.
Leading the collection are the Leaf Slides, perhaps a subtle nod to Anderson’s Dior Lily Pad heels. Embroidered green leather insoles mimic the striations of wide palm leaves you’d see swaying on the shore. Rendered in lime-colored nubuck leather, the insoles are also padded for indoor/outdoor comfort.
Other new footwear releases include basket-weave leather sandals like the Mule Slipper in sage green, black, and brown. Crafted in Italy, the mule has a nappa leather upper and a convertible back heel. For a closed-toe option, the new goat suede loafer is adorned with leather ruffles at the collar and matching suede laces.
The intricately basket-woven leather is also transferred to the JWA Loafer handbag silhouette, and other bags are comprised of woven raffia and colored beads that showcase JWA emblems. Along with the crocheted insect and iguana charms and other accessories, the Summer Series as a whole calls for a beach day!
Photo: JW Anderson
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by HYPEBEAST on April 23, 2026 tagging @jw_anderson, @hypebeaststyle, and @hypebeastkicks. May be an image of magazine, palm trees and text that says 'HYPEBEAST JW Anderson Is Putting Palm Trees on Your Feet'.

posts.caption:
The Mac Finder face you’ve clicked a thousand times just turned into a necklace.⁠
⁠
Designed by @delahuntagram, this “Finders Keepers” set is a take on the Finder icon, split into two stainless steel pendants. Keep both, or share it with someone who knows your shortcuts. Each half comes with its own chain, instantly recognizable if you’ve ever touched a Mac.⁠
⁠
Only 40 sets were made, priced at $199 with shipping included. Available now David Delahunty's website.⁠
Photo: David Delahunty
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by HYPEBEAST on April 23, 2026 tagging @delahuntagram. May be a meme of necklace and text that says 'HYPEBEAST The Finder Icon Just Got Turned Into a Necklace'.

posts.caption:
We’re at the @everyone.tokyo atelier, housed inside a Tadao Ando building, for a first look at the final chapter of their partnership with @adidasoriginals.
Building on that “Global for Local” theme, @ryomiyoshi and his team are leaning into a 2006 “Teamgeist” aesthetic for this drop. This includes oversized soccer jerseys, technical track pants, clean white slides, a waist pouch, and premium Italian suede reworks of the Adizero Adios OG in black and navy.
At this collaboration first look launch, Chef Kanchan from the modern Nepalese restaurant @adi_tokyo in Nakameguro is feeding guests with food, chai and wine stemmed from modern Himalayan cuisine.
The everyone x adidas Originals Vol. 2 collection lands on April 25.
Photo/Video: Ryan Kwok/Hypebeast

posts.caption:
@converse All Stars, but swapped out for Kyoto-made fabrics ⛩️⁠
⁠
This latest take links up with Kyoto textile workshop Ippuya, reworking the classic silhouette with traditional techniques like Kyo-Yuzen dyeing and chirimen crepe.⁠
⁠
Floral patterns run across the uppers, from camellia to cherry blossom and chrysanthemum while details like katakana ankle patches and woven labels point back to their Kyoto roots.⁠
⁠
Out now via ABC-MART in Japan.⁠
Photo: Converse

posts.caption:
Summer has a mood for every version of you—and @nidhhiagerwal knows exactly how to wear each one. Whether it’s the soft, golden haze of a slow morning or the unapologetic heat of high noon, she shifts between them the way only someone truly at ease in her own skin can. 🌸
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this becomes more than a seasonal study—it is a portrait of summer as emotion, texture, and transformation.
Light changes, moods shift, and she moves through it all with the kind of effortless cool that makes you want to rethink your entire wardrobe.
From dewy and dreamy to bold and sun-kissed, this is summer dressing as self-expression—textured, emotional, and completely her own.✨

posts.caption:
@umathurman Chooses Happiness: For decades, Thurman has embodied a certain kind of unstoppable presence, and  this month, the icon returns to the grisly genre that cemented her stardom, as a bloodthirsty avenger in #PrettyLethal. But off-screen things are less grim: Thurman is energized by her work and looking forward to the possibilities of her next chapter—one where she’s making space for new roles, new interests, and more time for herself: “I think the choice to be happy is one that should be made regardless of any conditions.”
Read more now at the link in @instylemagazine’s bio.

posts.caption:
Man of the Moment — Yashasvi Jaiswal (@yashasvijaiswal28) continues to rise, and we’re here for every second of it.
From dreaming of centuries in blue to turning those visions into reality and now hitting it out of the park as the opener for Rajasthan Royal (@rajasthanroyals) in IPL, his journey is the very definition of hard work meeting opportunity. Powered as much by an unshakable mindset as by his brilliance on the pitch, our cover star opens up about the drive, discipline, and hunger that fuel his remarkable ascent, “I don’t know where I’m going to go, but I want to enjoy the journey.”
Starting out as a cricketing prodigy, now 24, he’s all about keeping his head in the game, stopping every now and then, to savour the simple joys of life. And maybe that is the secret sauce to success, one game at a time.
Here’s raising a toast to the ‘player of the match’- Yashasvi Jaiswal!

posts.caption:
As a '94 kid, imma basically in that elite
"Zillennial" sweet spot
the literal bridge between the analog
childhood and the digital takeover. 🌉✨
Being part of that '96 batch was
a total vibe shift; imma right there when the
"fine print" of Gen Z culture was being
written in real time.
There is something so
iconic about that overlap. You’ve got the Millennial work ethic and the "gatekeeping our peace" energy, but you high key speak the language of the Gen Z girlies. It’s like being the "true vintage" original while still being the "final boss" of the current trend cycle. You aren't just watching the culture change; you helped reauthor it from the inside. That mix of Millennial resilience and Gen Z’s unhinged humor is the ultimate personality flex. 🥂💅🔥

you know the spicy "Toofani" kick

the brand, not the beverage." If we
can handle the "Beef" between generations

Stay unbothered and grab a glass-bottle local soda instead

posts.caption:
Nita Ambani at the @TIME100 Summit in New York, representing a lifetime of work shaped by purpose and a deep engagement with India’s cultural landscape.
She wore the Tribal Lore saree by Swadesh, a Jamdani masterpiece woven over 24 months by Padma Shri awardee Biren Kumar Basak in Phulia, West Bengal. Crafted in intricate meenakari Jamdani, the sari brings together tribal motifs, figurative storytelling, and auspicious fish borders, each element rendered with remarkable precision.
The pallu unfolds as a narrative tableau of ceremonial scenes and natural forms, while the body balances soft pastel stripes with detailed compositions. Anchored by fish motifs symbolising prosperity, the drape stands as a rare heirloom—where craft and storytelling converge.
#NitaAmbani  #RelianceAtTIME100  #TIME100  #TIME100Summit  #SwadeshAtTime100

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Quando la moda incontra il design, succede qualcosa di inaspettato. Durante la #MilanDesignWeek, @eleventy_milano porta in Via della Spiga 7 la sedia Farfalla di @truedesignofficial — una riedizione contemporanea di un’icona del 1974, riletta da Folco Orlandini. La farfalla non è solo un simbolo: era il primo logo del brand, e oggi torna a raccontare leggerezza, trasformazione, bellezza. In vetrina fino a fine aprile.
#Eleventy #TrueDesign #MilanDesignWeek2026 #FashionAndDesign

posts.caption:
More than a space, it is a narrative taking shape. In the heart of Milan, Salotto @Retori_official unveils itself as the debut flagship store for the brand born from the creative vision of @salmarachidm, but above all, as a sanctuary for encounter and vision. Here, fashion opens up to dialogue, evolving into a cultural language within an environment where art and beauty coexist and transform - restoring a living, shared dimension and configuring a new rhetoric of fashion.
Più che uno spazio, un racconto che prende forma. Nel cuore di Milano, il Salotto @Retori_official si presenta come il primo flagship store del marchio nato dalla visione creativa di @salmarachidm, ma soprattutto come un luogo di incontro e visione. Qui la moda si apre al dialogo, diventando linguaggio culturale in un ambiente in cui arte e bellezza convivono e si trasformano, restituendo una dimensione viva, condivisa e configurando una nuova retorica della moda.

posts.caption:
Corso Venezia 56. Sette piani, sette atmosfere, una sola idea di lusso vissuto. Ieri sera RH ha aperto le porte della sua prima Gallery italiana — e Milano ha risposto come sa fare: in grande. Tra i sette piani del palazzo c’erano tutti. @ZoeSaldana, Margot Robbie, @BrunelloCucinelli, Roberto Bolle, Anna Dello Russo — e una lista che dice tutto sulla serata. Il #design incontra il #cinema, l’arte incontra l’architettura, e Corso Venezia si conferma l’indirizzo che conta.

#RHMilano #MilanoDesignWeek #Luxury

posts.caption:
@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she’s still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠
“I enter every experience like it’s new, like I don’t know how it’s going to unfold,” she explains. “I come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I’m there, I can be flexible, not rigid.” Read Hudson’s full interview at the link in our bio. ⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
⁠While her approach to acting has always been instinctive and fluid, producing calls for a more structured mindset for @katehudson, who continues to expand her role behind the scenes as executive producer on ‘Running Point.’ ⁠
⁠
It’s also a natural next step in her career. Inspired by her mother, Goldie Hawn—one of the first actresses to produce her own films—Hudson explored producing early on, but stepped back after ‘Bride Wars,’ when she was a new mom and the timing didn’t feel right.⁠
⁠
As with everything else, she waited until she could fully commit—a quality that sets her up for a successful future as a producer. Read our full interview with Hudson at the link in our bio. ⁠
⁠
